{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = V31:n-t G14-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmut/Kenmet
''Knm.t'' }} '''Kenmut''' (auch '''Kenmet''') war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung des mittleren Teils vom insgesamt zehn [[Stern]]e umfassenden ersten Dekan-[[Sternbild]]es, das aus [[Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|Wasserschlange]] und [[Löwe (Sternbild)|Löwe]] bestand. == Kenmut in der ägyptischen Mythologie == In der [[Prädynastik (Ägypten)|prädynastischen Zeit]] erfolgte um den 19. bis 20. Juni der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang des Gesamtsternbildes Kenmut mit dem Eintreffen der [[Nilschwemme]] im [[Nildelta]]. Die Ägypter nannten diesen Moment: ''Kenmut wird geboren''. Nach dem Ende der Nilschwemme und der folgenden [[Aussaat]] im Monat [[Ka-her-ka]] begann ''am Himmel seine Regierungszeit als [[Dekade (Altägypten)|arbeitender Dekan des Kenmut]]''. Kenmuts symbolischer Tod in den Monaten [[Chenti-chet]] und [[Ipet-hemet (Monat)|Ipet-hemet]] signalisierte das Ende der Erntesaison. Bis zur ''Wiedergeburt der nächsten Nilschwemme'' war Kenmut unsichtbar zum Schutz der Toten im [[Duat]]. == Wechsel in der Dekanreihenfolge == [[Sesostris III.]] ordnete in seinem siebten Regierungsjahr die Reihenfolge der Dekane neu, da infolge der [[Präzession]] der [[Erde]] und der [[Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|Eigenbewegung]] ''Kenmuts Geburt'' auf den 15. Juli gewandert war. Das vorher aus drei Dekaden bestehende Sternbild wurde auf nur noch sieben Sterne reduziert und auf zwei Dekaden verteilt. Kenmut ''betrat mit seinem Tod den Duat'' nun erst am Ende des ägyptischen Jahres und hielt sich dort während der einsetzenden Nilschwemme auf. Die Funktion als ''Auslöser der Nilflut'' hatte zwischenzeitlich [[Sopdet]] übernommen. == Erscheinungsformen == Die [[Schlangen#Symbolik und Mythologie|Schlange]] symbolisierte im Alten Ägypten das neu entstehende Leben und die Wiedergeburt. Kenmuts Darstellung als ''Geburtsdekan'' erfolgte daher in Form einer Großen Schlange, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wurde. In der Funktion als Schöpfungsgottheit sowie als Fährmann in der Barke des Duat, der die Toten begleitet, verschmolz Kenmut mit [[Chnum|Chnoumis]]. Mit [[Horus]] von [[Letopolis]] (nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) verkörperte Kenmut als falkenköpfiger Mensch die Zeit der Aussaat. Später kam die weitere Identifikation mit [[Sachmet]] hinzu, da Kenmut mit seiner ''Geburt nach der Nilschwemme'' auch als Überbringer von gefährlichen Seuchen galt. == Literatur == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{Navigationsleiste Ägyptische Astronomie}} [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Astronomie]] [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Mythologie]]

Die Funktion als ''Auslöser der Nilflut'' hatte zwischenzeitlich [[Sopdet]] übernommen. == Erscheinungsformen == Die [[Schlangen#Symbolik und Mythologie|Schlange]] symbolisierte im Alten Ägypten das neu entstehende Leben und die Wiedergeburt. Kenmuts Darstellung als ''Geburtsdekan'' erfolgte daher in Form einer großen Schlange, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wurde. In der Funktion als Schöpfungsgottheit sowie als Fährmann in der Barke des Duat, der die Toten begleitet, verschmolz Kenmut mit [[Chnum|Chnoumis]]. Mit [[Horus]] von [[Letopolis]] (nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) verkörperte Kenmut als falkenköpfiger Mensch die Zeit der Aussaat. Später kam die weitere Identifikation mit [[Sachmet]] hinzu, da Kenmut mit seiner ''Geburt nach der Nilschwemme'' auch als Überbringer von gefährlichen Seuchen galt. == Literatur == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = V31:n-t G14-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmut / Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''Die vom [[Leopard|Panther]]'' }} '''Kenmut''' (auch '''Kenmet''') war ursprünglich die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung des zweiten [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekans]] vom insgesamt zehn [[Stern]]e umfassenden Drei-Dekan-[[Sternbild]]es des [[Achet|ersten Achet-Monats]]. Es bestand aus den [[Tierkreiszeichen]] [[Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|Wasserschlange]] und [[Löwe (Sternbild)|Löwe]]. == Kenmut in der ägyptischen Mythologie == In der [[Prädynastik (Ägypten)|prädynastischen Zeit]] erfolgte um den 19. bis 20. Juni der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang des Gesamtsternbildes Kenmut mit dem Eintreffen der [[Nilschwemme]] im [[Nildelta]]. Die Ägypter nannten diesen Moment: ''Kenmut wird geboren''. Nach dem Ende der Nilschwemme und der folgenden [[Aussaat]] im Monat [[Ka-her-ka]] begann ''am Himmel seine Regierungszeit als arbeitender Dekan des Kenmut''. Kenmuts symbolischer Tod in den Monaten [[Chenti-chet]] und [[Ipet-hemet (Monat)|Ipet-hemet]] signalisierte das Ende der Erntesaison. Bis zur ''Wiedergeburt der nächsten Nilschwemme'' war Kenmut unsichtbar zum Schutz der Toten im [[Duat]]. == Wechsel in der Dekanreihenfolge == [[Sesostris III.]] ordnete in seinem siebten Regierungsjahr die Reihenfolge der Dekane neu, da infolge der [[Präzession]] der [[Erde]] und der [[Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|Eigenbewegung]] ''Kenmuts Geburt'' auf den 15. Juli gewandert war. Das vorher aus drei Dekaden bestehende Sternbild wurde auf nur noch sieben Sterne reduziert und auf zwei Dekaden verteilt. Kenmut ''betrat mit seinem Tod den Duat'' nun erst am Ende des ägyptischen Jahres und hielt sich dort während der einsetzenden Nilschwemme auf. Die Funktion als ''Auslöser der Nilflut'' hatte zwischenzeitlich [[Sopdet]] übernommen. == Erscheinungsformen == Die [[Schlangen#Symbolik und Mythologie|Schlange]] symbolisierte im Alten Ägypten das neu entstehende Leben und die Wiedergeburt. Kenmuts Darstellung als ''Geburtsdekan'' erfolgte daher in Form einer großen Schlange, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wurde. In der Funktion als Schöpfungsgottheit sowie als Fährmann in der Barke des Duat, der die Toten begleitet, verschmolz Kenmut mit [[Chnum|Chnoumis]]. Mit [[Horus]] von [[Letopolis]] (nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) verkörperte Kenmut als falkenköpfiger Mensch die Zeit der Aussaat. Später kam die weitere Identifikation mit [[Sachmet]] hinzu, da Kenmut mit seiner ''Geburt nach der Nilschwemme'' auch als Überbringer von gefährlichen Seuchen galt. == Literatur == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]] [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Mythologie]]

''Knm.t''
''Die von [[Kenmet (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Kenmet]]'' }} '''Kenmet''' (auch '''Kenmut''') war ursprünglich die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung des zweiten [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekans]] vom insgesamt zehn [[Stern]]e umfassenden Drei-Dekan-[[Sternbild]]es des [[Achet|ersten Achet-Monats]]. Es bestand aus den [[Tierkreiszeichen]] [[Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|Wasserschlange]] und [[Löwe (Sternbild)|Löwe]]. == Kenmet in der ägyptischen Mythologie == In der [[Prädynastik (Ägypten)|prädynastischen Zeit]] erfolgte um den 19. bis 20. Juni der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang des Gesamtsternbildes Kenmut mit dem Eintreffen der [[Nilschwemme]] im [[Nildelta]]. Die Ägypter nannten diesen Moment: ''Kenmut wird geboren''. Nach dem Ende der Nilschwemme und der folgenden [[Aussaat]] im Monat [[Ka-her-ka]] begann ''am Himmel seine Regierungszeit als arbeitender Dekan des Kenmut''. Kenmuts symbolischer Tod in den Monaten [[Chenti-chet]] und [[Ipet-hemet (Monat)|Ipet-hemet]] signalisierte das Ende der Erntesaison. Bis zur ''Wiedergeburt der nächsten Nilschwemme'' war Kenmut unsichtbar zum Schutz der Toten im [[Duat]]. == Wechsel in der Dekanreihenfolge == [[Sesostris III.]] ordnete in seinem siebten Regierungsjahr die Reihenfolge der Dekane neu, da infolge der [[Präzession]] der [[Erde]] und der [[Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|Eigenbewegung]] ''Kenmuts Geburt'' auf den 15. Juli gewandert war. Das vorher aus drei Dekaden bestehende Sternbild wurde auf nur noch sieben Sterne reduziert und auf zwei Dekaden verteilt. Kenmut ''betrat mit seinem Tod den Duat'' nun erst am Ende des ägyptischen Jahres und hielt sich dort während der einsetzenden Nilschwemme auf. Die Funktion als ''Auslöser der Nilflut'' hatte zwischenzeitlich [[Sopdet]] übernommen. == Erscheinungsformen == Die [[Schlangen#Symbolik und Mythologie|Schlange]] symbolisierte im Alten Ägypten das neu entstehende Leben und die Wiedergeburt. Kenmuts Darstellung als ''Geburtsdekan'' erfolgte daher in Form einer großen Schlange, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wurde. In der Funktion als Schöpfungsgottheit sowie als Fährmann in der Barke des Duat, begleitet er die Toten. Mit [[Horus]] von [[Letopolis]] (nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) verkörperte Kenmut als falkenköpfiger Mensch die Zeit der Aussaat. Später kam die weitere Identifikation mit [[Sachmet]] hinzu, da Kenmut mit seiner ''Geburt nach der Nilschwemme'' auch als Überbringer von gefährlichen Seuchen galt. == Literatur == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]] [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Mythologie]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]]

The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:new pages]]

Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]]

The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]]

''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. ''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kenmet (Decan)-qq]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:ppp]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:ppp]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]]

''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]]

It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:11 deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11 deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]][[Category:11 deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11 deprecated open]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]]
