{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=draft|12=draft}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft|12=draft}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern|12=draft|12=draft}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|12=draft|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=draft|12=draft}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|12=draft|12=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|12=draft|12=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|12=draft|12=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]]

As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|12=draft|12=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|12=draft|12=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=draft|12=draft}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft|12=draft}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern|12=draft|12=draft}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|12=draft|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=draft|12=draft}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|12=draft|12=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|12=draft|12=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|12=draft|12=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]]

The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=draft|12=draft}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|12=draft|12=draft}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=draft|12=draft}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|12=draft|12=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|12=draft|12=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|12=draft|12=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]]

Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ja import failed]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=draft}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern|12=draft}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen|12=draft}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)|12=draft}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=draft}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta|12=draft}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=draft}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|12=draft}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=draft}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|12=draft}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|12=draft}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|12=draft}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|12=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|12=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|12=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|12=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|12=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} [[Category:Pages with German translation box]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages without German translation box]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|12=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} [[Category:Pages with German translation box]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duat|qq=}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Precession|qq=}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|qq=}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Proper motion (astronomy)|qq=}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet|qq=}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horus|qq=}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letopolis|qq=}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Memphis (Egypt)|qq=}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekhmet|qq=}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egypt|qq=}} designation of the second {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decade (Ancient Egypt)|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constellation|qq=}} of the {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achet|qq=}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|de|Stern|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Star|qq=}}s. It included the {{ill|Zodiac sign|de|Tierkreiszeichen|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zodiac sign|qq=}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|de|Wasserschlange (Sternbild)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hydra (constellation)|qq=}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|de|Löwe (Sternbild)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Leo (constellation)|qq=}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Inundation of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inundation of the Nile|qq=}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|de|Nildelta|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nile Delta|qq=}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month {{ill|Ka-her-ka|de|Ka-her-ka|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ka-her-ka|qq=}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|de|Duat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duat|qq=}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|de|Präzession|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Precession|qq=}} of the {{ill|Earth|de|Erde|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|qq=}} and {{ill|Proper motion (astronomy)|de|Eigenbewegung (Astronomie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Proper motion (astronomy)|qq=}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet|qq=}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horus|qq=}} of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letopolis|qq=}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Memphis (Egypt)|qq=}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sachmet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekhmet|qq=}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]]

As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[en:draft:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|de|Schlange|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]]

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]]

Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]]

As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1632718]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Decan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}}
