{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1632718]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|qq=}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Non matching pagename on wikidata]]

Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|comment=no wikipedias work|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Matching pagename on wikidata]]

It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qq=}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|qq=}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|qq=}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|qq=}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1632718]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|WD=Q1738774}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|WD=Q714601}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|WD=Q777671}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|WD=Q1070110}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|WD=Q1672177}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Matching pagename on wikidata]]

As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1632718]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|WD=Q1738774}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|WD=Q714601}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|WD=Q777671}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|WD=Q1070110}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|WD=Q1672177}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|WD=Q714601}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|WD=Q777671}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|WD=Q1070110}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|WD=Q1672177}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1632718]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|WD=Q1738774}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] designation of the second [[:en:Decan|Decan]] of the three-decade [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] of the [[:en:Season of the Inundation|Achet]]'s first month, comprising ten [[:en:Star|Star]]s. It included the [[:en:Astrological sign|Zodiac sign]] [[:en:Hydra (constellation)|Hydra (constellation)]] and [[:en:Leo (constellation)|Leo (constellation)]]. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|WD=Q714601}} period, around June 19–20, the [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|Inundation of the Nile]] in the [[:en:Nile Delta|Nile Delta]]. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|WD=Q777671}} began in the month [[:en:Koiak|Ka-her-ka]], ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|WD=Q1070110}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|WD=Q1672177}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the [[:en:Duat|Duat]]. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]]'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the [[:en:Precession|Precession]] of the [[:en:Earth|Earth]] and [[:en:Proper motion|Proper motion (astronomy)]] had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]]. == Manifestations == The [[:en:Snake|Snake]] symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As [[:en:Horus|Horus]] of [[:en:Letopolis|Letopolis]] (northwest of [[:en:Memphis, Egypt|Memphis (Egypt)]]), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with [[:en:Sekhmet|Sekhmet]], as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} {{draft categories| }} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|WD=Q777671}} began in the month {{ill|Koiak|en|Koiak|WD=Q1410257|lt=Ka-her-ka}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|WD=Q1070110}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|WD=Q1672177}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|en|Precession|WD=Q104225|lt=Precession}} of the {{ill|Earth|en|Earth|WD=Q2|lt=Earth}} and {{ill|Proper motion|en|Proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=Proper motion (astronomy)}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|WD=Q84122|lt=Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|en|Letopolis|WD=Q1013648|lt=Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis (Egypt)}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|en|Sekhmet|WD=Q146104|lt=Sekhmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]

[[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31-S3-G14:X1-N14-N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Kenmet
''Knm.t''
''The one of {{ill|Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|de|Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)|WD=Q1738774|1=Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)}}'' }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut''') was originally the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}} designation of the second {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|WD=Q5248738|lt=Decan|1=Decan}} of the three-decade {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation|1=Constellation}} of the {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|WD=Q770568|lt=Achet|1=Season of the Inundation}}'s first month, comprising ten {{ill|Star|en|Star|WD=Q523|lt=Star|1=Star}}s. It included the {{ill|Astrological sign|en|Astrological sign|WD=Q1795024|lt=Zodiac sign|1=Astrological sign}} {{ill|Hydra (constellation)|en|Hydra (constellation)|WD=Q10578|lt=Hydra (constellation)|1=Hydra (constellation)}} and {{ill|Leo (constellation)|en|Leo (constellation)|WD=Q8853|lt=Leo (constellation)|1=Leo (constellation)}}. == Kenmet in Egyptian Mythology == During the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|WD=Q714601|1=Predynastic Egypt}} period, around June 19–20, the {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}} of the entire constellation Kenmut coincided with the arrival of the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|WD=Q1519552|lt=Inundation of the Nile|1=Flooding of the Nile}} in the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|WD=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta|1=Nile Delta}}. The Egyptians referred to this moment as: ''Kenmut is born.'' After the inundation ended and the subsequent {{ill|Sowing|de|Aussaat|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sowing|WD=Q777671|1=Sowing}} began in the month {{ill|Koiak|en|Koiak|WD=Q1410257|lt=Ka-her-ka|1=Koiak}}, ''his reign as a working decan of Kenmut in the sky began.'' Kenmut’s symbolic death during the months {{ill|Chenti-chet|de|Chenti-chet|1=Khenti-khet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chenti-chet|13=User:Immanuelle/Khenti-khet|WD=Q1070110}} and {{ill|Ipet-hemet (month)|de|Ipet-hemet (Monat)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipet-hemet (month)|WD=Q1672177|1=Ipet-hemet (month)}} marked the end of the harvest season. Until the ''rebirth of the next inundation,'' Kenmut remained invisible, protecting the dead in the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|WD=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}}. == Change in Decan Sequence == In the seventh year of {{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III|1=Senusret III}}'s reign, the sequence of decans was rearranged because the {{ill|Precession|en|Precession|WD=Q104225|lt=Precession|1=Precession}} of the {{ill|Earth|en|Earth|WD=Q2|lt=Earth|1=Earth}} and {{ill|Proper motion|en|Proper motion|WD=Q193223|lt=Proper motion (astronomy)|1=Proper motion}} had shifted ''Kenmut’s birth'' to July 15. The previously three-decade constellation was reduced to seven stars and distributed over two decades. Kenmut ''entered the Duat with his death'' only at the end of the Egyptian year and stayed there during the beginning of the inundation. Meanwhile, the function as ''trigger of the Nile flood'' was transferred to {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet|1=Sopdet}}. == Manifestations == The {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}} symbolized new life and rebirth in Ancient Egypt. Kenmut was depicted as a ''birth decan'' in the form of a large snake crossed by three smaller snakes. In his role as a creation deity and ferryman on the Duat’s barque, he accompanied the dead. As {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|WD=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}} of {{ill|Letopolis|en|Letopolis|WD=Q1013648|lt=Letopolis|1=Letopolis}} (northwest of {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|WD=Q5715|lt=Memphis (Egypt)|1=Memphis, Egypt}}), Kenmut embodied the time of sowing as a falcon-headed man. Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|en|Sekhmet|WD=Q146104|lt=Sekhmet|1=Sekhmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|WD=Q2643704|1=Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 {{translated page|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|version=163691392|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Kenmet (Dekan)]] [[ja:ケンムト]] {{wikidata link|Q1632718}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:Egyptian mythology]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]

Later, he was also identified with {{ill|Sekhmet|en|Sekhmet|qid=Q146104|lt=Sekhmet|1=Sekhmet}}, as Kenmut’s ''birth after the inundation'' symbolized his role as a bringer of dangerous plagues. == Literature == * Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens'', Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X}} * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qid=Q2643704|1=Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''.
